content: If we reframe biological pathogenic agents (from chemistry/biology definitions of Topic B) as memory-augmented Markov decision processes, we can model how pathogens adapt their infection trajectories based on past host immune responses they have 'remembered' from prior encounters or mutational history. This perspective treats pathogen infectivity not as a static trait, but as a sequential decision-making process where stored memory of past host environments shapes the pathogen's next action.
